original_abstract: Speech brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) offer a transformative pathway for restoring communication to individuals with paralysis by translating neural signals associated with attempted speech into text or synthesized sound. While early iterations showed promise, they struggled to maintain accuracy when decoding unconstrained, large-vocabulary sentences. In this study, we present a high-performance speech-to-text BCI utilizing intracortical microelectrode arrays to record single-neuron activity. Our participant, who lost the ability to speak intelligibly due to amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), achieved a 9.1% word error rate on a 50-word vocabulary—a nearly three-fold improvement over previous benchmarks. More notably, we achieved a 23.8% word error rate on a 125,000-word vocabulary, marking what we believe is the first successful demonstration of large-vocabulary decoding. The system reached a decoding speed of 62 words per minute, surpassing the prior record by a factor of 3.4 and moving closer to the pace of natural conversation. Our findings further reveal two critical properties of the neural speech code: the spatial intermixing of tuning for speech articulators, which allows for high-fidelity decoding from small cortical areas, and the long-term preservation of detailed phonetic representations years after the onset of paralysis. Collectively, these advancements demonstrate a viable clinical path toward restoring rapid, fluent communication for those who have lost the power of speech.
humanized_abstract: Speech BCIs represent a new approach to enable persons with paralysis to communicate through conversing by translating their speech-related neural activity into words or synthetic speech. Previous speech BCI studies that relied on brain-computed auditory speech features or on electrocerebral activity have generally failed to attain reliable and high accuracy speech recognition for open vocabulary and unconstrained sentences. Here, we introduce a high performance speech to text BCI that uses intracortical recordings with a microelectrode array to capture activity of individual neurons corresponding to speech-related articulator movements, and is capable of achieving fast and accurate transcription of speech. Our paralysed participant, an ALS patient who lost the ability to speak purposefully and meaningfully, achieved a 9.1% word error rate on the 50-word “WER50” test—three times better than the previous best speech BCI performance on open vocabulary speech recognition. He also achieved a 24% word error rate on the WER125k—what we believe to be a first in large vocabulary BCI-based speech recognition performance. His BCI could achieve a speech recognition rate of 62 words per minute, 3.4 times faster than the previous BCI performance, which is within an order of magnitude of the fast speech rate in humans. We also identified two salient features of speech neural code underlying the high BCI performance in terms of the spatial distribution of speech-related neural activity as well as the long term persistence of speech phone features post-paralysis. Our work provides a feasible solution for persons with paralysis to restore the ability to communicate efficiently and effectively through speech.
